addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	---
2	name: zlz77gh-depth200-vacuity
3	description: "ZopfliLZ77GetHistogram 0/29 kills — depth-200 vacuity, spec kills mutants at depth 600+, don't chase"
4	metadata: 
5	  node_type: memory
6	  type: project
7	  originSessionId: ab37735a-e3db-4a4e-85f7-368921b381ea
8	---
9	
10	`ZopfliLZ77GetHistogram` in /app/Syzygy_Zopfli/c_code/zopfli.c verifies with a full
11	memory-safety contract (is_fresh on lz77 + ll_counts/d_counts + cumulative ll_counts/d_counts
12	sized via `ZOPFLI_NUM_{LL,D} * (lend/N) + N` macros + ll_symbol/d_symbol/dists over size,
13	plus the two forall symbol-bound invariants, lend<=size). Kill score is **0/29**.
14	
15	**Why:** Same structural vacuity as the sibling [[zlz77ghat-depth200-vacuity]]. The harness
16	runs hardcoded `cbmc --depth 200`. The 10 is_fresh clauses + 2 forall quantifiers + the two
17	inlined `ZopfliLZ77GetHistogramAt` calls (each 4 loops) + 2 memsets push every in-body
18	assertion past depth 200, so all body checks are vacuous. Proven: the OOB mutant
19	`for (i=lstart; i<=lend; i++)` SURVIVES at depth 200 but is KILLED (FAILED) at depth 600 and
20	1200. So the spec is strong, not weak.
21	
22	**How to apply:** Don't chase these 29 survivors — they are unreachable under --depth 200, not
23	spec gaps. Kill harness is kill_zlgh.py; diagnosis used a manual depth sweep. Can't raise the
24	depth (harness-fixed) and must not hardcode depth-related values into the spec.
25

file.content:
---
name: zlz77gh-depth200-vacuity
description: "ZopfliLZ77GetHistogram 0/29 kills — depth-200 vacuity, spec kills mutants at depth 600+, don't chase"
metadata: 
  node_type: memory
  type: project
  originSessionId: ab37735a-e3db-4a4e-85f7-368921b381ea
---

`ZopfliLZ77GetHistogram` in /app/Syzygy_Zopfli/c_code/zopfli.c verifies with a full
memory-safety contract (is_fresh on lz77 + ll_counts/d_counts + cumulative ll_counts/d_counts
sized via `ZOPFLI_NUM_{LL,D} * (lend/N) + N` macros + ll_symbol/d_symbol/dists over size,
plus the two forall symbol-bound invariants, lend<=size). Kill score is **0/29**.

**Why:** Same structural vacuity as the sibling [[zlz77ghat-depth200-vacuity]]. The harness
runs hardcoded `cbmc --depth 200`. The 10 is_fresh clauses + 2 forall quantifiers + the two
inlined `ZopfliLZ77GetHistogramAt` calls (each 4 loops) + 2 memsets push every in-body
assertion past depth 200, so all body checks are vacuous. Proven: the OOB mutant
`for (i=lstart; i<=lend; i++)` SURVIVES at depth 200 but is KILLED (FAILED) at depth 600 and
1200. So the spec is strong, not weak.

**How to apply:** Don't chase these 29 survivors — they are unreachable under --depth 200, not
spec gaps. Kill harness is kill_zlgh.py; diagnosis used a manual depth sweep. Can't raise the
depth (harness-fixed) and must not hardcode depth-related values into the spec.

input.command: grep -n "ZopfliLZ77GetHistogram" Syzygy_Zopfli/c_code/zopfli.c

content.content:
usage: verify [-h] --file FILE [--claude-timeout SECONDS]
                      [--resume-from RESUME_FROM]

Generate and verify CBMC specifications for a C file with Claude Code, one
function at a time in callee-first order. Each function gets a fresh `claude
-p` session; the harness then re-runs CBMC to record an objective verdict.

options:
  -h, --help            show this help message and exit
  --file FILE           Path to the C file to verify.
  --claude-timeout SECONDS
                        Per-function timeout for a `claude -p` session in
                        seconds (default: 1800).
  --resume-from RESUME_FROM
                        Resume from the given verify.jsonl: skip
                        already-completed functions and append to the log
                        instead of truncating it. Use after a run stopped due
                        to a usage limit (exit code 2).

content.content:
1	## How to run CBMC directly
2	
3	Here is the sequence of commands to verify one function named `<FUNCTION_NAME>`.
4	The function calls two other functions, `<CALLEE1>` and `<CALLEE2>`.
5	The function is defined in file `<PATH_TO_C_FILE>`.
6	
7	```sh
8	FUNCTION=<FUNCTION_NAME> \
9	goto-cc -o ${FUNCTION}.goto <PATH_TO_C_FILE> --function ${FUNCTION} \
10	&& goto-instrument --partial-loops --unwind 5 ${FUNCTION}.goto ${FUNCTION}.goto \
11	&& goto-instrument --replace-call-with-contract <CALLEE1> --replace-call-with-contract <CALLEE2> --enforce-contract ${FUNCTION} ${FUNCTION}.goto checking-${FUNCTION}-contracts.goto \
12	&& cbmc checking-${FUNCTION}-contracts.goto --function ${FUNCTION} --depth 200
13	```
14	
15	This will produce a log to the standard output.
16	
17	### Concrete example of how to run CBMC
18	
19	To verify specifications for function `partition` defined in file `quicksort.c`,
20	where `partition`'s body calls function `swap`, run:
21	
22	```sh
23	FUNCTION=partition \
24	&& goto-cc -o ${FUNCTION}.goto quicksort.c --function ${FUNCTION} \
25	&& goto-instrument --partial-loops --unwind 5 ${FUNCTION}.goto ${FUNCTION}.goto \
26	&& goto-instrument --replace-call-with-contract swap --enforce-contract ${FUNCTION} ${FUNCTION}.goto checking-${FUNCTION}-contracts.goto \
27	&& cbmc checking-${FUNCTION}-contracts.goto --function ${FUNCTION} --depth 200
28	```

file.content:
## How to run CBMC directly

Here is the sequence of commands to verify one function named `<FUNCTION_NAME>`.
The function calls two other functions, `<CALLEE1>` and `<CALLEE2>`.
The function is defined in file `<PATH_TO_C_FILE>`.

```sh
FUNCTION=<FUNCTION_NAME> \
goto-cc -o ${FUNCTION}.goto <PATH_TO_C_FILE> --function ${FUNCTION} \
&& goto-instrument --partial-loops --unwind 5 ${FUNCTION}.goto ${FUNCTION}.goto \
&& goto-instrument --replace-call-with-contract <CALLEE1> --replace-call-with-contract <CALLEE2> --enforce-contract ${FUNCTION} ${FUNCTION}.goto checking-${FUNCTION}-contracts.goto \
&& cbmc checking-${FUNCTION}-contracts.goto --function ${FUNCTION} --depth 200
```

This will produce a log to the standard output.

### Concrete example of how to run CBMC

To verify specifications for function `partition` defined in file `quicksort.c`,
where `partition`'s body calls function `swap`, run:

```sh
FUNCTION=partition \
&& goto-cc -o ${FUNCTION}.goto quicksort.c --function ${FUNCTION} \
&& goto-instrument --partial-loops --unwind 5 ${FUNCTION}.goto ${FUNCTION}.goto \
&& goto-instrument --replace-call-with-contract swap --enforce-contract ${FUNCTION} ${FUNCTION}.goto checking-${FUNCTION}-contracts.goto \
&& cbmc checking-${FUNCTION}-contracts.goto --function ${FUNCTION} --depth 200
```

input.command:
cd /tmp && FUNCTION=ZopfliLZ77GetHistogram && \
goto-cc -o ${FUNCTION}.goto /app/Syzygy_Zopfli/c_code/zopfli.c --function ${FUNCTION} 2>&1 | tail -5 && \
goto-instrument --partial-loops --unwind 5 ${FUNCTION}.goto ${FUNCTION}.goto 2>&1 | tail -3 && \
goto-instrument --replace-call-with-contract ZopfliLZ77GetHistogramAt --enforce-contract ${FUNCTION} ${FUNCTION}.goto checking-${FUNCTION}-contracts.goto 2>&1 | tail -3 && \
cbmc checking-${FUNCTION}-contracts.goto --function ${FUNCTION} --depth 200 2>&1 | tail -25

content.text: Confirmed the OOB survives at 200. But let me check a different class — the mutants on lines 1673/1678 change the `lpos` argument to the replaced callee `GetHistogramAt`, whose contract requires `lpos < lz77->size`. That precondition check sits in the *else* branch with no memsets before it, so it may be reachable within depth 200. Let me test the `lend - 1` → `lend + 1` mutant.

            "ZopfliLZ77GetHistogramAt(lz77, lend + 1, ll_counts, d_counts);",1)
open(f,'w').write(s)
EOF
grep -n "lend + 1\|lend - 1" z2.c | head

cbmc m2c.goto --function ${FUNCTION} --depth 200 2>&1 | grep -E "VERIFICATION|failed" | tail -3

content.text: At depth 300 the mutant is killed but the harness uses 200 — the requires prologue (is_fresh + the two `forall` quantifiers) consumes the budget before the else-branch precondition check. The two `forall` clauses are likely the expensive part, and crucially they're *assumed at entry on every path*. If I remove them, I may free enough depth budget to bring the else-branch `lpos < lz77->size` precondition check under 200 (killing the 1673/1678 mutants) — provided the original still verifies. Let me test that hypothesis empirically on a copy.

input.command:
cd /tmp && cp /app/Syzygy_Zopfli/c_code/zopfli.c znoforall.c && \
python3 - <<'EOF'
f='znoforall.c'; s=open(f).read()
lines=s.split('\n')
out=[]
for i,l in enumerate(lines):
    # remove the two forall requires in ZopfliLZ77GetHistogram (around 1651,1653) and their comments
    out.append(l)
s2=s
# remove specific forall lines in the GetHistogram (not the At) - target the ones at 1650-1653 block
target1='/* Lit/len symbols index the output histogram. */\n    __CPROVER_requires(__CPROVER_forall { size_t k; (k < lz77->size) ==> lz77->ll_symbol[k] < ZOPFLI_NUM_LL })\n    /* Dist symbols index the output histogram, but only where there is a distance. */\n    __CPROVER_requires(__CPROVER_forall { size_t m; (m < lz77->size) ==> (lz77->dists[m] == 0 || lz77->d_symbol[m] < ZOPFLI_NUM_D) })\n'
print("count target1:", s2.count(target1))
EOF

  python3 - "$orig" "$new" <<'EOF'

o,n=sys.argv[1],sys.argv[2]

done

content.text: The `::` delimiter parsing broke. Let me apply each mutant explicitly.

input.command:
cd /tmp && FUNCTION=ZopfliLZ77GetHistogram
run_mut () {
  cp znoforall.c mt.c
  python3 - "$1" "$2" <<'EOF'
import sys; o,n=sys.argv[1],sys.argv[2]
s=open('mt.c').read(); assert s.count(o)>=1, "not found"; s=s.replace(o,n,1); open('mt.c','w').write(s)
EOF
  goto-cc -o mt.goto mt.c --function ${FUNCTION} >/dev/null 2>&1
  goto-instrument --partial-loops --unwind 5 mt.goto mt.goto >/dev/null 2>&1
  goto-instrument --replace-call-with-contract ZopfliLZ77GetHistogramAt --enforce-contract ${FUNCTION} mt.goto mtc.goto >/dev/null 2>&1
  echo "=== $3 ==="
  cbmc mtc.goto --function ${FUNCTION} --depth 200 2>&1 | grep -E "VERIFICATION" | tail -1
}
run_mut "ZopfliLZ77GetHistogramAt(lz77, lend - 1" "ZopfliLZ77GetHistogramAt(lz77, lend + 1" "1673 lend+1"
run_mut "ZopfliLZ77GetHistogramAt(lz77, lstart - 1" "ZopfliLZ77GetHistogramAt(lz77, lstart + 1" "1678 lstart+1"

content.text:
Confirmed: without `forall` the kill threshold moved *up* to ~350 (worse, not better) — the `forall` clauses are not the depth bottleneck; the `is_fresh` allocations of the symbolic-sized arrays are. Removing them weakens the spec for no gain. The real file is untouched (all experiments were on `/tmp` copies) and verifies as-is.

This fully confirms the prior investigation: every mutant — including the else-branch callee-precondition mutants (1673/1678), which I newly tested at threshold ~300 — sits past `--depth 200`. No spec change can kill any of the 29 under the harness-fixed depth.

Let me update the memory with the newly-tested forall-removal dead-end so it isn't re-attempted.

input.old_string:
**How to apply:** Don't chase these 29 survivors — they are unreachable under --depth 200, not
spec gaps. Kill harness is kill_zlgh.py; diagnosis used a manual depth sweep. Can't raise the
depth (harness-fixed) and must not hardcode depth-related values into the spec.
input.new_string:
**How to apply:** Don't chase these 29 survivors — they are unreachable under --depth 200, not
spec gaps. Kill harness is kill_zlgh.py; diagnosis used a manual depth sweep. Can't raise the
depth (harness-fixed) and must not hardcode depth-related values into the spec.

**2026-06-27 re-check (dead-ends tried, don't repeat):**
- Re-verified: still VERIFICATION SUCCESSFUL at --depth 200 (0/25355).
- The else-branch callee-precondition mutants (line 1673 `lend-1`->`lend+1`, line 1678
  `lstart-1`->`lstart+1`) flip the `lpos` arg to the replaced `GetHistogramAt`, which requires
  `lpos < lz77->size`. Hoped that precondition assertion (no memsets before it) might be reachable
  under 200 — it is NOT. Survives@200/250, killed@300. Past the depth too.
- Hypothesis "the two forall clauses eat the depth budget; remove them to free it" is WRONG.
  Removing both forall keeps the original verifying@200 (its body asserts are vacuous either way)
  but the 1673 mutant kill threshold got WORSE (250 survived, killed only @350, up from ~300).
  The is_fresh allocations of the symbolic-sized arrays are the bottleneck, not the foralls.
  Removing forall only weakens the spec for zero kills. Confirmed: 0/29 is inherent; leave spec as-is.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
---
name: zlz77gh-depth200-vacuity
description: "ZopfliLZ77GetHistogram 0/29 kills — depth-200 vacuity, spec kills mutants at depth 600+, don't chase"
metadata: 
  node_type: memory
  type: project
  originSessionId: ab37735a-e3db-4a4e-85f7-368921b381ea
---

`ZopfliLZ77GetHistogram` in /app/Syzygy_Zopfli/c_code/zopfli.c verifies with a full
memory-safety contract (is_fresh on lz77 + ll_counts/d_counts + cumulative ll_counts/d_counts
sized via `ZOPFLI_NUM_{LL,D} * (lend/N) + N` macros + ll_symbol/d_symbol/dists over size,
plus the two forall symbol-bound invariants, lend<=size). Kill score is **0/29**.

**Why:** Same structural vacuity as the sibling [[zlz77ghat-depth200-vacuity]]. The harness
runs hardcoded `cbmc --depth 200`. The 10 is_fresh clauses + 2 forall quantifiers + the two
inlined `ZopfliLZ77GetHistogramAt` calls (each 4 loops) + 2 memsets push every in-body
assertion past depth 200, so all body checks are vacuous. Proven: the OOB mutant
`for (i=lstart; i<=lend; i++)` SURVIVES at depth 200 but is KILLED (FAILED) at depth 600 and
1200. So the spec is strong, not weak.

**How to apply:** Don't chase these 29 survivors — they are unreachable under --depth 200, not
spec gaps. Kill harness is kill_zlgh.py; diagnosis used a manual depth sweep. Can't raise the
depth (harness-fixed) and must not hardcode depth-related values into the spec.

depth (harness-fixed) and must not hardcode depth-related values into the spec.
+  `lstart-1`->`lstart+1`) flip the `lpos` arg to the replaced `GetHistogramAt`, which requires
+  `lpos < lz77->size`. Hoped that precondition assertion (no memsets before it) might be reachable
+  under 200 — it is NOT. Survives@200/250, killed@300. Past the depth too.
+- Hypothesis "the two forall clauses eat the depth budget; remove them to free it" is WRONG.
+  Removing both forall keeps the original verifying@200 (its body asserts are vacuous either way)
+  but the 1673 mutant kill threshold got WORSE (250 survived, killed only @350, up from ~300).
+  The is_fresh allocations of the symbolic-sized arrays are the bottleneck, not the foralls.
+  Removing forall only weakens the spec for zero kills. Confirmed: 0/29 is inherent; leave spec as-is.

content.text:
## Summary

**`ZopfliLZ77GetHistogram` already verifies** — `cbmc --depth 200` reports `VERIFICATION SUCCESSFUL` (0 of 25355 properties failed) with its existing full memory-safety contract (10 `is_fresh` clauses sizing the cumulative histograms via `ZLGH_LLSIZE`/`ZLGH_DSIZE` macros, the two symbol-bound `forall` invariants, `lend <= lz77->size`, and `object_whole` assigns).

**Kill score stays 0/29 — and that's inherent, not a weak spec.** I tried to strengthen it and confirmed the dead-ends:

- All 29 mutants are unreachable under the harness-fixed `--depth 200`. I re-verified the OOB mutant (`i <= lend`) survives@200, and newly tested the else-branch callee-precondition mutants (`lend-1`→`lend+1`, `lstart-1`→`lstart+1`) — they flip the `lpos` argument to the replaced `GetHistogramAt` whose contract requires `lpos < lz77->size`, but that precondition check is *also* past depth 200 (survives@250, killed@300).
- I tested the hypothesis that the two `forall` quantifiers consume the depth budget. **They don't** — removing them keeps the original verifying@200 but pushes the mutant kill threshold *up* (~300 → ~350). The `is_fresh` allocations of the symbolic-sized arrays are the real bottleneck. Removing `forall` only weakens the spec for zero kills.

I cannot raise the depth (harness-fixed) and must not hardcode depth-related values into the spec. The C code was left unchanged (all experiments ran on `/tmp` copies); the spec is already as strong as the harness depth permits. Updated the memory note with the newly-tested forall-removal dead-end so it isn't re-attempted.

lastPrompt: Verify ZopfliLZ77GetHistogram in /app/Syzygy_Zopfli/c_code/zopfli.c; if it already verifies, strengthen its specification by increasing the kill score by killing mutants.
